_comment: Source of truth for the SQLite content library. seed_db.py loads this and does INSERT OR REPLACE, so re-running it is always safe. difficulty must be one of: beginner, intermediate, academic. Add more entries here to scale the bank -- no code changes needed.

sentences: Every morning, I stop by the coffee shop on my way to work.
The line is usually long, but it moves pretty fast.
I always order a medium coffee with a little milk.
The barista knows my name and my order by now.
It costs about three dollars, which adds up over time.
Still, that first sip makes the whole day feel easier.
Excuse me, could you tell me how to get to the train station?
Sure, go straight down this street for two blocks.
Then turn left at the traffic light by the bank.
You'll see the station entrance right across from the park.
It's about a ten minute walk from here.
You can't miss it — there's a big clock above the doors.
Hi, I'd like to return this book, please.
No problem, I'll scan it right now.
It looks like it's two days late.
That's fine, the fine is only twenty cents a day.
Would you like a receipt for that?
No thanks, that's okay.
Our professor suggested we form study groups before the midterm.
At first, I wasn't sure it would actually help me.
I usually prefer to review the material on my own.
But explaining concepts to other people turned out to be powerful.
When you teach something, you quickly discover what you don't understand.
By the end of the semester, our group met twice a week.
My grade improved, and I made three good friends along the way.
Before any interview, research the company for at least an hour.
Look at their website, their products, and their recent news.
Prepare two or three thoughtful questions to ask at the end.
Interviewers remember candidates who seem genuinely curious.
Practice your answers out loud, not just in your head.
Speaking the words changes how confident they sound in the room.
Many cities are planting thousands of trees along their streets.
Trees provide shade, which lowers summer temperatures significantly.
They also absorb rainwater and reduce the risk of flooding.
Studies show that green streets encourage people to walk more.
Property values often rise in neighborhoods with mature trees.
For a relatively small cost, trees deliver decades of benefits.
When the bill came, nobody wanted to deal with the math.
We ended up using an app to split it evenly.
It calculated everyone's share, including tax and tip.
One friend had only ordered water, so we adjusted his share down.
The whole process took less than a minute.
It saved us from an awkward conversation at the table.
Human memory doesn't work like a video recording of events.
Instead, each time we recall something, we partially reconstruct it.
That reconstruction can be influenced by our current emotions and beliefs.
This is why two people remember the same conversation so differently.
Researchers have even implanted entirely false memories in laboratory settings.
Understanding this fragility should make us more humble about certainty.
It also explains why eyewitness testimony is less reliable than juries assume.
When my landlord announced a rent increase, I decided to negotiate.
I gathered listings for comparable apartments in the neighborhood.
Then I politely pointed out that I'd never paid late in three years.
Reliable tenants are valuable, and replacing one is expensive.
I proposed a smaller increase in exchange for signing a longer lease.
To my surprise, she accepted the offer almost immediately.
The whole conversation took fifteen minutes and saved me six hundred dollars.
The university library will extend its hours during final examination week.
Beginning Monday, all three floors will remain open until two in the morning.
Additional staff will be available at the research help desk each evening.
Students are reminded to bring their identification cards for late entry.
Free coffee and snacks will be provided in the lobby after ten.
The administration hopes these changes will reduce stress during finals.
Psychologists have long studied why people hesitate to help in emergencies.
One explanation is called the bystander effect.
The more witnesses present, the less likely any single person is to act.
Each bystander assumes someone else will take responsibility instead.
Researchers demonstrated this using staged emergencies in public spaces.
Awareness of the effect alone has been shown to reduce it somewhat.
Simply knowing the pattern exists can make people more likely to step in.
I needed a ladder to clean out my gutters last weekend.
My neighbor Tom has a whole shed full of tools.
I knocked on his door and asked if I could borrow one.
He said sure, as long as I brought it back by Sunday.
The ladder was heavier than I expected.
When I returned it, I brought him a few cookies to say thanks.
It started raining just as I left the house.
I didn't have an umbrella, so I pulled up my hood.
The streets were almost empty except for a few other walkers.
Puddles had formed along the sidewalk near the corner shop.
By the time I got home, my shoes were completely soaked.
Still, there was something calming about the sound of the rain.
A few neighbors decided to turn an empty lot into a garden.
First, they asked the city for permission to use the space.
Then they cleared away weeds and old trash left over the years.
Volunteers built raised beds out of donated wood.
Each family who joined got a small section to plant.
By midsummer, the garden was producing tomatoes, herbs, and beans.
It became a place where people who barely knew each other started talking.
When I finished school, I wasn't sure what career to pursue.
My parents wanted me to study something practical, like engineering.
But I felt more drawn to writing and design.
I decided to take a year to try a few internships.
One internship at a small design studio really clicked with me.
It wasn't the safest choice, but it felt like the right one.
A few years later, I still don't regret following that instinct.
Advertisers spend enormous amounts of money studying how people make decisions.
One common technique is creating a sense of urgency around a purchase.
Phrases like 'limited time only' push shoppers to act before they think it through.
Another strategy relies on associating a product with a desirable lifestyle.
Researchers have found that repeated exposure alone increases a brand's perceived trustworthiness.
Understanding these techniques doesn't make consumers immune to them, but it does make people more cautious.
Being aware of the pattern is often the first step toward more deliberate spending.
Psychologists have found that people form impressions of others within seconds of meeting them.
These snap judgments are based on facial expressions, posture, and tone of voice.
Once an impression forms, people tend to notice information that confirms it.
This means a poor first impression can be surprisingly difficult to reverse.
Interviewers, for example, often decide how they feel about a candidate before the interview truly begins.
Some researchers suggest deliberately pausing before judging someone to counter this bias.
Simply knowing the tendency exists can make people slightly more fair-minded in their evaluations.

toefl_reading.passage: Coral reefs form when tiny marine animals called polyps build hard skeletons from calcium carbonate. Over {{b1}} of years, these skeletons {{b2}} on top of each other, gradually {{b3}} into massive reef structures. Reefs {{b4}} shallow, warm, and sunlit water, since the algae living inside coral polyps {{b5}} sunlight to produce energy. In {{b6}}, reefs provide shelter for an enormous {{b7}} of marine species, {{b8}} they cover less than one percent of the ocean floor. Unfortunately, rising water temperatures can cause coral to {{b9}} its algae, a process known as bleaching, which threatens the {{b10}} of entire reef ecosystems.

items.explanation: "Over ___ of years" needs a large plural time unit.
Skeletons build up layer on layer over time.
A gerund is needed after "gradually" to describe the ongoing process.
Reefs need these specific conditions to exist.
Algae need sunlight for photosynthesis, matching the reason clause that follows.
"In fact" is a fixed transitional phrase introducing an elaboration.
"An enormous ___ of marine species" calls for variety or range.
Introduces the contrast between small area and enormous biodiversity.
Corals push out (expel) their algae during bleaching.
Bleaching threatens whether reef ecosystems continue to exist.

toefl_reading.set_title: The Invention of the Printing Press
toefl_reading.passage: In the 1400s, Johannes Gutenberg developed a printing press that used movable metal type. Before this {{b1}}, books had to be copied by {{b2}}, which made them expensive and {{b3}}. Gutenberg's press allowed identical pages to be {{b4}} quickly and cheaply, {{b5}} the number of books available across Europe. As literacy {{b6}}, ordinary people gained access to ideas that had once been {{b7}} to scholars and clergy. Some historians {{b8}} that the printing press helped spark the Renaissance and the Reformation by {{b9}} the rapid spread of new ideas. Few inventions have had such a lasting {{b10}} on how humans share knowledge.

items.explanation: Refers back to the press just described.
"Copied by hand" is a fixed expression for manual copying.
Pairs with "expensive" to describe scarce, hard-to-obtain books.
Matches "pages ... to be ___" in the context of a printing press.
Participle phrase describing the result of printing quickly and cheaply.
Matches "as literacy ___" -- literacy becoming more widespread.
Contrasts with "gained access," meaning access was previously restricted.
Historians commonly "argue that" when presenting a claim.
Participle explaining how the press helped spark change.
"Lasting ___ on" is a common collocation meaning lasting effect.

toefl_reading.passage: Many bat species hunt for insects in total darkness using a technique called echolocation. As a bat {{b1}}, it emits high-pitched sounds that humans cannot {{b2}}. These sound waves travel through the air until they {{b3}} an object, such as a moth, and bounce back toward the bat. By {{b4}} the time it takes for the echo to {{b5}}, the bat can judge the object's distance with remarkable {{b6}}. Some bats can even {{b7}} between a smooth surface and a textured one based on how the echo changes. This {{b8}} ability lets bats navigate cluttered spaces and {{b9}} tiny, fast prey without relying on {{b10}} at all.

items.explanation: "As a bat ___" needs a subject-appropriate verb matching "emits".
The sounds are too high-pitched for the human ear to hear.
Sound waves hitting an object, matching "bounce back" that follows.
A gerund is needed after the preposition "by".
Matches "for the echo to ___" -- the echo returning to the bat.
"With remarkable ___" pairs with judging distance accurately.
"Can even ___ between X and Y" is a fixed pattern.
Echoes the earlier description of the ability as impressive.
Bats catching prey, paired with "navigate" as a parallel verb.
Contrasts with echolocation -- bats don't need vision.

toefl_reading.passage:
Subject: You're Registered!

Dear Priya,

This confirms your registration for ENG 210: Introduction to Linguistics, meeting Tuesdays and Thursdays at 2:00 PM starting September 8th. Please purchase the course textbook before the first class. If you need to drop the course, you must do so by September 12th to receive a full refund. Contact the registrar's office with any questions.

Best,
Office of the Registrar

items.question_text: What is the main purpose of this email?
items.explanation: The email opens by confirming Priya's registration for ENG 210.

items.question_text: According to the email, what should Priya do before the first class?
items.explanation: The email instructs her to purchase the textbook before the first class.

items.question_text: What does the email say happens if Priya drops the course after September 12th?
options: She does not receive a full refund
items.explanation: A full refund requires dropping by September 12th, implying no full refund after that date.

toefl_reading.passage:
NOTICE: New Study Room Policy

Starting Monday, all group study rooms on the third floor must be reserved online at least two hours in advance. Walk-in bookings are no longer accepted. Reservations are limited to two hours per group per day. Rooms not claimed within 15 minutes of the reservation time will be released to the next group on the waitlist.

items.question_text: What is the main purpose of this notice?
options: To announce new study room hours
To explain a new room reservation policy
To advertise a new library building
To ask students to donate books
items.explanation: The notice explains the new online reservation requirement for study rooms.

items.question_text: What happens if a group doesn't claim its reserved room within 15 minutes?
options: The room is given to the next group waiting
The group is charged a fee
The room stays empty for the day
items.explanation: The notice states unclaimed rooms are released to the next group on the waitlist.

toefl_reading.passage:
Photography Club Meetup this Friday!

Join us at 5 PM in the Student Union, Room 214, for a beginner-friendly workshop on night photography. Bring your own camera or phone -- we'll cover a few basics before heading outside to shoot the campus at sunset. Free snacks provided. Drop a comment if you're coming so we know how many chairs to set up!

items.question_text: What is this post mainly about?
options: Inviting students to a photography workshop
items.explanation: The post invites students to a Friday photography workshop.

items.question_text: What does the post ask readers to do if they plan to attend?
items.explanation: The post asks readers to comment if they're coming, to plan seating.

toefl_reading.passage:
When patients are given a treatment they believe is effective, they sometimes improve even if the treatment has no active medical ingredient. This phenomenon, known as the placebo effect, has puzzled researchers for decades. In clinical trials, one group of patients often receives an actual drug while another receives a placebo, typically a sugar pill with no therapeutic properties. Remarkably, patients who receive the placebo frequently report reduced pain, improved mood, or fewer symptoms, even though they have taken nothing that should produce these changes physiologically.

Researchers believe the placebo effect arises from a combination of expectation and conditioning. When a patient expects a treatment to work, the brain can release natural chemicals, such as endorphins, that genuinely reduce the sensation of pain. Prior experience also plays a role: a patient who has been helped by pills in the past may unconsciously associate the act of taking a pill with feeling better, regardless of its contents.

The placebo effect complicates medical research because it means any new treatment must outperform a placebo, not simply produce improvement on its own. At the same time, understanding the placebo effect has practical value: some doctors now study how communication, ritual, and expectation can be used ethically to support conventional treatment, rather than replace it.

items.question_text: According to the passage, what is a placebo?
options: A drug with strong side effects
A substance with no therapeutic properties
items.explanation: Paragraph 1 defines a placebo as 'typically a sugar pill with no therapeutic properties.'

items.question_text: According to the passage, all of the following are mentioned as possible placebo effects EXCEPT
items.explanation: The passage mentions reduced pain, improved mood, and fewer symptoms, but never wound healing.

items.question_text: The word 'conditioning' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to
options: A learned association through repeated experience
items.explanation: 'Conditioning' refers to associating pill-taking with feeling better through past experience -- a learned association.

items.question_text: It can be inferred from the passage that new medical treatments are tested against placebos in order to
options: Reduce the cost of clinical trials
Determine whether a treatment works better than expectation alone
Avoid the need for government approval
items.explanation: Paragraph 3 states a treatment must outperform a placebo, implying trials isolate the treatment's effect from expectation alone.

items.question_text: Why does the author mention endorphins in paragraph 2?
options: To criticize the use of placebos in research
To explain a possible biological mechanism behind the placebo effect
To argue that placebos are more effective than real drugs
To describe a side effect of modern medicine
items.explanation: The mention of endorphins supports the explanation of how expectation could produce a real physiological change.

toefl_reading.passage:
Cities are often several degrees warmer than the surrounding countryside, a phenomenon known as the urban heat island effect. This temperature difference occurs because materials commonly used in cities, such as asphalt and concrete, absorb and retain heat far more effectively than natural surfaces like grass, soil, or forest canopy. During the day, these materials soak up solar energy, and at night they slowly release that stored heat back into the air, preventing urban areas from cooling down as quickly as rural ones.

Several factors intensify the effect. Tall buildings can trap heat at street level by blocking wind that would otherwise carry it away. Vehicle traffic and air conditioning units release additional heat directly into the surrounding air. Meanwhile, the removal of trees and green space for construction eliminates natural shading and the cooling effect of plant evaporation.

Urban heat islands are not merely uncomfortable; they carry real public health risks, particularly during heat waves, when nighttime temperatures fail to drop enough to give residents relief. In response, some cities have begun installing reflective or 'cool' roofing materials, expanding tree cover, and designing parks specifically to interrupt concrete surfaces and reintroduce green space into dense neighborhoods.

items.question_text: According to the passage, why do cities retain more heat than surrounding areas?
options: Cities use materials that absorb and hold heat
Cities are located at higher elevations
items.explanation: Paragraph 1 explains that asphalt and concrete absorb and retain heat more than natural surfaces.

items.question_text: In paragraph 1, what does the word 'that' in 'they slowly release that stored heat' refer to?
options: Solar energy absorbed during the day
items.explanation: 'That stored heat' refers back to the solar energy the materials soaked up during the day.

items.question_text: Which sentence in paragraph 2 explains how the loss of trees contributes to the heat island effect?
options: "Tall buildings can trap heat at street level by blocking wind that would otherwise carry it away."
"Vehicle traffic and air conditioning units release additional heat directly into the surrounding air."
"Meanwhile, the removal of trees and green space for construction eliminates natural shading and the cooling effect of plant evaporation."
"Urban heat islands are not merely uncomfortable; they carry real public health risks."
items.explanation: This sentence directly links tree removal to the loss of shading and evaporative cooling.

items.question_text: Look at the four positions in paragraph 3 that indicate where the following sentence could be added: 'Even a few degrees of difference can make heat waves significantly more dangerous.' Position 1 is right before 'Urban heat islands are not merely uncomfortable'; Position 2 is right after 'give residents relief'; Position 3 is right after 'cool roofing materials'; Position 4 is right after 'interrupt concrete surfaces'. Where would the sentence best fit?
items.explanation: The inserted sentence elaborates on why the health risk matters, fitting right after the sentence describing that risk.

items.question_text: Why does the author mention 'cool' roofing materials in the final paragraph?
options: To criticize cities for reacting too slowly
To give an example of how cities are responding to the problem
To argue that technology cannot solve the heat island effect
To compare rooftops in different countries
items.explanation: The final paragraph lists cool roofing as one of several city responses to the heat island effect.

toefl_reading.passage:
Dogs were the first animal species domesticated by humans, likely tens of thousands of years before any other. Exactly how this domestication began remains debated among scientists, but one widely accepted theory suggests that wolves gradually domesticated themselves. According to this view, wolves that were less fearful of humans began lingering near early settlements to scavenge discarded food. Over many generations, natural selection favored the calmer, less aggressive wolves that tolerated human presence, since they had reliable access to food scraps that more anxious wolves avoided.

As these animals became increasingly comfortable around people, humans likely began to notice practical benefits: the animals could alert them to approaching danger and assist with tracking prey. Selective breeding, whether intentional or accidental, gradually produced animals that were smaller, more sociable, and more attuned to human behavior than their wild ancestors.

Genetic evidence broadly supports this timeline, though researchers still disagree about precisely where and how many times domestication occurred. What is clear is that the relationship transformed both species: dogs evolved traits suited to living alongside humans, while humans gained a companion whose skills and loyalty would prove useful across thousands of years of shared history.

items.question_text: According to the passage, what is one widely accepted theory about how dogs were domesticated?
options: Humans captured and tamed wild wolf pups
Wolves gradually domesticated themselves by tolerating human presence
Humans bred dogs specifically for hunting from the start
items.explanation: The passage describes the self-domestication theory in which less fearful wolves lingered near settlements.

items.question_text: The word 'scavenge' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to
options: To search for and collect discarded food
To share equally among a group
items.explanation: In context, wolves 'scavenge discarded food,' meaning they search for and gather leftover food.

items.question_text: Which of the following best expresses the essential information in this sentence from the passage: 'Over many generations, natural selection favored the calmer, less aggressive wolves that tolerated human presence, since they had reliable access to food scraps that more anxious wolves avoided.'
options: Calmer wolves were favored by natural selection because they could reliably access food near humans.
Anxious wolves were favored because they avoided humans entirely.
Natural selection had no effect on which wolves survived near humans.
Wolves competed with humans for food scraps over many generations.
items.explanation: The simplified version keeps the core idea -- calmer wolves survived better through reliable food access -- while dropping secondary detail.

items.question_text: It can be inferred from the passage that researchers currently
options: Agree completely on when and where dogs were first domesticated
Still debate some specific details of dog domestication despite general agreement on the process
Believe dogs were domesticated more recently than genetic evidence suggests
Have found no genetic evidence related to domestication
items.explanation: Genetic evidence 'broadly supports this timeline,' but researchers 'still disagree about precisely where and how many times domestication occurred.'

items.question_text: In paragraph 2, what does the word 'their' in 'more attuned to human behavior than their wild ancestors' refer to?
items.explanation: 'Their wild ancestors' refers back to the animals (dogs) being described as more sociable than their wild ancestors (wolves).

toefl_writing.context_line: The professor said our essays are due by Friday at noon.
toefl_writing.explanation: A polite yes/no question asking which submission method to use, following naturally from the professor's deadline statement. 'already' is an extra word not used.

toefl_writing.context_line: I'm not sure if the campus shuttle runs on weekends.
toefl_writing.explanation: A hedged statement ('I think') giving a likely answer to the uncertainty raised in the first line. 'maybe' is an extra word not used.

toefl_writing.context_line: Are you going to the study group tonight?
toefl_writing.explanation: A conditional response: attendance depends on finishing the assignment first. 'already' is an extra word not used.

toefl_writing.context_line: Didn't you say the bookstore was having a sale this week?
toefl_writing.explanation: Confirms the sale ('Yes,') while adding a contrasting detail about its deadline ('but it ends...').

toefl_writing.context_line: I can't find my student ID card anywhere.
toefl_writing.explanation: Practical advice using 'should' to recommend an action in response to the problem. 'quickly' is an extra word not used.

toefl_writing.context_line: The Wi-Fi in the library has been really slow lately.
toefl_writing.explanation: A softened suggestion ('Maybe we should') proposing a next step in response to the complaint.

toefl_writing.context_line: I heard the guest lecture got moved to a bigger room.
toefl_writing.explanation: A natural follow-up question asking for the missing detail (the new room). 'actually' is an extra word not used.

toefl_writing.context_line: My roommate wants to switch to a different meal plan.
toefl_writing.explanation: An agreement statement using the common pattern 'That sounds like...' to react positively to the news.

toefl_writing.context_line: The advisor's office said walk-ins are only allowed on Tuesdays.
toefl_writing.explanation: A decision statement ('I'll just...') offering a practical alternative given the walk-in restriction. 'maybe' is an extra word not used.

toefl_writing.context_line: I can't believe how expensive the new textbook is.
toefl_writing.explanation: A practical question suggesting a cheaper alternative (checking the library) in response to the complaint about cost.

toefl_writing.situation: You are on the volunteer committee for a campus charity fun run. A new student, Marco, emailed the committee asking how he could get involved.
toefl_writing.email_prompt:
Write an email to Marco. In your email, do the following:
- Explain what the fun run is and when it takes place.
- Describe one specific way he could help (for example, the registration table, route marshaling, or event setup).
- Encourage him to sign up and tell him how.
Write as much as you can and in complete sentences.

toefl_writing.situation: Your study group's regular meeting room is being renovated starting next week. You need to suggest a new place to meet to your groupmate, Dana.
toefl_writing.email_prompt:
Write an email to Dana. In your email, do the following:
- Explain the problem with the current meeting room.
- Suggest a specific alternative location and time.
- Ask whether the new plan works for her.
Write as much as you can and in complete sentences.

toefl_writing.situation: You noticed that a professor's syllabus for a class you both are interested in lists no prerequisites, but your friend Alex is nervous the class might be too advanced for a beginner.
toefl_writing.email_prompt:
Write an email to Alex. In your email, do the following:
- Reassure Alex about taking the class.
- Give a specific reason why you think it would be manageable.
- Suggest one thing you could both do together to prepare.
Write as much as you can and in complete sentences.

toefl_writing.situation: Your dorm's laundry machines have been broken for two weeks. You want to report the issue to the housing office contact, Ms. Chen, and request a fix.
toefl_writing.email_prompt:
Write an email to Ms. Chen. In your email, do the following:
- Describe the problem with the laundry machines and how long it has been going on.
- Explain how the problem is affecting you and your dormmates.
- Request a specific action or timeline for a fix.
Write as much as you can and in complete sentences.

toefl_writing.situation: You borrowed detailed lecture notes from a classmate, Priya, before an exam, and they helped you a lot. You want to thank her and offer to return the favor.
toefl_writing.email_prompt:
Write an email to Priya. In your email, do the following:
- Thank her for lending you the notes.
- Mention one specific way the notes helped you on the exam.
- Offer to help her with something in return.
Write as much as you can and in complete sentences.

toefl_writing.situation: A new international student, Yuki, emailed the campus newcomers' club asking for tips on adjusting to campus life. You are a club officer replying.
toefl_writing.email_prompt:
Write an email to Yuki. In your email, do the following:
- Give two or three specific pieces of practical advice for adjusting to campus life.
- Mention one resource or place on campus that helped you personally.
- Invite her to an upcoming club event.
Write as much as you can and in complete sentences.

toefl_writing.professor_prompt: Your discussion topic this week: should universities require every student to take at least one course outside their major field? Post your view and support it with reasons.

classmate_posts.name: Jordan
classmate_posts.text: I think outside courses are great. My required art history class completely changed how I think about design in my own engineering projects. You never know what will end up being useful.

classmate_posts.name: Priya
classmate_posts.text: I disagree. My schedule is already packed with major requirements, and an unrelated course just means less time for the classes that actually prepare me for my career.

toefl_writing.professor_prompt: Your discussion topic this week: is it better for students to work part-time jobs during their studies, or to focus solely on academics? Share your opinion and explain your reasoning.

classmate_posts.name: Marcus
classmate_posts.text: I work about 15 hours a week at a campus cafe, and honestly it taught me time management skills that my classes never did. I get more done in less time now because I have to.

classmate_posts.name: Elena
classmate_posts.text: I tried working during my first semester and my grades dropped noticeably. I think students should focus on academics first and get work experience through internships later, when the pressure is different.

toefl_writing.professor_prompt: Your discussion topic this week: should classroom attendance be mandatory, or should students be free to skip class as long as they still pass the exams? Give your view.

classmate_posts.name: Diego
classmate_posts.text: I don't think attendance should be forced. I learn better from reading and recorded lectures at my own pace, and mandatory attendance just penalizes people whose learning style is different.

classmate_posts.name: Hana
classmate_posts.text: I actually think attendance matters more than people admit. In-class discussions bring up points that never make it into the textbook, and skipping class means missing that entirely.

toefl_writing.professor_prompt: Your discussion topic this week: do group projects prepare students better for the workplace than individual assignments do? Explain your position.

classmate_posts.name: Tyler
classmate_posts.text: Group projects are supposed to build teamwork skills, but in my experience one or two people always end up doing most of the work. I don't think that reflects real workplace collaboration.

classmate_posts.name: Aisha
classmate_posts.text: I've had the opposite experience. My best group projects taught me how to divide tasks, negotiate deadlines, and handle disagreements, which are exactly the skills my internship supervisor said mattered most.

toefl_writing.professor_prompt: Your discussion topic this week: should universities ban laptops and phones during lectures to improve focus? Share your view and support it.

classmate_posts.name: Noah
classmate_posts.text: I use my laptop to take notes far faster than I can by hand, and I can search past notes instantly before exams. Banning devices would actually hurt students like me.

classmate_posts.name: Sofia
classmate_posts.text: I switched to handwritten notes last semester after noticing how often I got distracted by messages, and I remember the material so much better now. I think the distraction cost outweighs the convenience.

toefl_writing.professor_prompt: Your discussion topic this week: is it more valuable for students to specialize deeply in one subject, or to gain broad knowledge across many subjects? Give your opinion.

classmate_posts.name: Liam
classmate_posts.text: Specializing is what actually gets you hired. Employers in my field want deep, specific expertise, not a general overview of ten different subjects that barely scratches the surface of any of them.

classmate_posts.name: Grace
classmate_posts.text: I'd push back on that. The most interesting solutions I've seen in my classes came from people connecting ideas across different fields, something a narrow specialist would never think to try.
